transcript_text:
Okay, so Evan, maybe start by telling me a bit about yourself. What are you currently doing? Do you work, do you study? What year of study, if you are studying? Where do you live... that kind of thing.
So, I am a first-year at Southampton Solent University. I'm currently studying sports journalism, and I'm 19.
Nice! And what does a typical week look like for you, then?
So, a typical week is... I'm in three days a week in lectures, and then I also play for the university American football team. So, I've got normally a game at the weekend and then training once or twice in the midweek.
Nice. Okay, that's pretty cool... extracurricular. What about time-tail structure? Do you have some busy days, some lighter days?
Last semester, it used to be pretty busy, and some of the... my lectures were split up, but now they're all... they're all four-hour lectures. So I've got one on a Monday, one on a Wednesday, and one on a Thursday.
Okay, and so... it's just four hours, you're in, and you're done?
Yes. Yeah.
Okay, that's great! And where do you currently live, and who with?
So, I currently live in student halls, in the residences. I live with, oh, seven other people in my little flat, but six of them... no, five of them... that I'm living in a house with next year.
Okay, great! So, you've already sorted that?
Yes.
So, how do you normally make decisions around food in the... in the flat? And do you all cook and buy groceries together, or is everything done separately?
We... most of the time, we do our own things. I think some of the girls sometimes group up, but most of the time, I'm... I like to sort of do my own thing and work around what I'm doing.
That's perfect. Yeah... What about social media, then? What social media platforms are you currently on?
I have pretty much all of them, to be honest. I, I use Snapchat, Instagram, TikTok... probably the most. YouTube... I watch a lot of that. X, or Twitter, or whatever it's called now, I use for... because I'm in sports journalism, so I have to constantly stay up to date with all the stuff that's going on.
Yeah, perfect. And, roughly, how much time would you say you spend on social media in a typical day?
Probably too much... Probably three or four hours, I reckon.
Okay, that's great! Where would you tend to see the most food-related content, then?
I reckon Instagram or TikTok... I think there's always videos on my, like, 'For You' page about recipes or takeaways, promoting something great!
And did you follow any food influencers, creators, or local food accounts? I...
I don't think I do... but they come up quite a lot.
Okay... have you ever, then, ordered food because you've seen it on social media or visited a restaurant?
I think probably subconsciously... Yeah. I think when you get, like, the McDonald's or another takeaway advert come up, and, like, you haven't got much in the cupboard, I'm thinking, yeah, I might as well order one.
And is that as... as you're scrolling on Instagram?
Yes... yeah, yeah. Bit of a guilty habit to be.
That's fair enough. And was... was it the promotion that enticed you to order, or was it just a reminder of the food?
A bit of both, to be honest. Like, I thought that it's, like, easy and I could just get it in the, like, next 15 minutes instead of going all the way to the shop and buying, like, ingredients and stuff.
That's great, thank you! Okay, walk me through a typical weekday then, from morning to night... What do you eat? When and where?
So I'll wake up... or most of my lectures are at 9 a.m., apart from one of them. So I wake up probably too late, about half eight-ish. Sometimes I grab, like, a waffle or, like, a bagel in the kitchen on my way to uni, and then I'll do my lecture. Sometimes I'll buy, like, I don't know, a snack at uni, like crisps or something. And then when I get back, I'll usually eat. I normally eat a lot of pasta, so that's probably that. Maybe some dough balls, and then I'll probably, I don't know, faff around for a bit. And then whenever it gets to tea time, I'll either have stuff in the cupboard, or I just go out or order something.
Nice. Okay, and how would you say that differs from the weekends?
I think on the weekends, normally, because I do my food shop, I'm probably a bit more organized, and I've got more time to actually think about what I'm going to eat instead of just doing it on the fly from when I come back from university.
Nice. Okay. And what is it that you kind of look for, or what's your, like, main drivers when you're deciding on what to eat?
Normally... do I like it? It's probably the main thing, like the price. And is it going to be an easy, quick thing, or is it going to take me, like, a good half an hour, 45 minutes? Because then it will depend on what I'm doing that day. Like, if I've got training—because I train quite late at night—I'll probably go for something more like a ready meal than trying to cook up a whole...
Makes sense, I suppose, on that... What would you say cooking actually means to you?
I'd say cooking is probably like using ingredients and, like, making something your own instead of just, like, putting a ready meal in the microwave.
Nice... okay, so, like, something from scratch.
Yes... yeah, yeah.
Okay... and how often, then, would you say you order takeaway and groceries for delivery?
Well, I order... Well, my mum actually orders my food shopping. We do that about once a week. And then I'll... I try and limit my takeaways, but probably maybe one or two a week.
Okay, when you say your mum orders the... the groceries, where does she order them from? Is it like a... like a fast delivery app? Like Uber delivery?
Tesco delivery... so, like, through the app.
Nice. Okay... And is that your only grocery shop? It's just like one big shop a week?
That's the majority of it. Like the odd bits that, like, get substituted or, like, are out of stock, I go and get, like... I don't know if I need more milk or eggs or that kind of thing, but the majority comes from the Tesco.
Perfect. Okay, so... which meals, then, do you feel are most stressful?
Probably breakfast, because, like, I know I should be eating it, but I'm not a morning person, so I never really get up in time. And then I'm like, oh, I probably should have had something to eat before I went to uni, but haven't really got enough time... So I say probably that one.
Perfect. Okay, and which meals feel most enjoyable, then, to contrast that... probably?
Like tea or dinner in the evening... because, like, I know apart from one day, one or two days a week, I know I'm done for the day, and I can take time and have whatever I'd like to eat, basically.
Nice. Okay, and then when would you say food feels: 1, functional; 2, social; 3, comforting?
It's really nice when we have quite a lot of us in the kitchen, whether that's like cooking or just talking at the same time... because occasionally, there'll be like five or six of us all in the kitchen cooking at the same time. And that's got quite a nice social feel!
Nice. Okay, so roughly how much do you think you spend on food in a typical week? So, that's everything... that's takeouts, groceries, and eating out.
I reckon, probably in the region of about 120 pounds a week.
Okay, fantastic. And how would you split that, then, between those three categories... between food, takeaways?
Groceries and eating out... I'd say groceries probably take up the largest majority, and then sort of takeaways, and then eating out after that.
Okay... do you have, like, a rough estimate of how much you could spend?
I tried to spend, like, no more than sort of 20-ish quid on takeaway... the week. And then eating out? Maybe sort of about the same.
Okay, great. So, 60 quid for groceries, and then 40 between the other two?
Yes... yeah, great!
And do you have a set budget for each week, or does that change?
I tried to have a set budget, but I'm not very good at sort of, like, organizing everything. So, like, if it happens, it sort of happens, but I try and remain in sort of the, like, 120, 130th sort of ballpark.
Absolutely. Okay, so what would you say is the... the main reason you normally order a takeaway?
Probably convenience. And I normally do it after my... because I have a lot of, like, away games, so I have to go to, like... I can't remember where the last one was, like Surrey and up to, like, the other side of London, really. So I, like... I'll get back, and then I'm like, I can't really be bothered to cook. So I just have a look on the takeaway apps, and probably just all the sunning. Great!
Okay. And is that a particular day? Is there a particular day of the week you're more likely to order, normally?
On a Sunday, because that's when my games are... or probably like a Wednesday or Thursday, because I've got a lot of stuff going on that day.
Okay, great! I'd love for you, then, to walk me through the last time you ordered a takeaway for delivery. So, walk me through what triggered it... Who were you with? What were you doing, ultimately? What led to you deciding to place an order via one of the apps: Uber Delivery or Just Eat?
Yeah, so last time I ordered was about a week and a half ago. It was after my training, so it was Wednesday night, and I finished training... It's eight to 10, so I finished training at 10, and I have to get the bus back with my mates. And I sat next to my mate, and he was... We were talking about what we was... get tweaks. There's a shop right next to the bus stop, and he was like, 'Oh, I just think I'm going to order something.' And then that's all, like, planted a seed in my head. And I was like, 'Oh, well, I could just order something, you know, it's been raining... but long day. Just got back from training. I think I might just order something.' And I usually use UberEats because I've got a free promotion for, like, the student Uber One thing. So I use that, try and get some money off and... Yeah!
Nice. Okay. And do you ever order as a group, or is it normally just, you know, solo?
It's normally just solo... yeah.
Okay, great. So, what kind of foods, then, do you... do you typically order? And are there, like, go-to orders, or is it about variety for you?
There's... I normally have, like... I've got to go to, like... I normally go. I normally order, like, a McDonald's or a Wendy's or a Five Guys sometimes. Those are normally my three. I don't really go outside those three. Those are normally my favorites.
Nice... Okay. Do you think your ordering habits have changed over time?
I'd say so, because when I was at home, I lived like in the middle of nowhere in a very rural town. So, like, and there's pretty much no... I mean, there's takeaways, but you have to go and drive to get them. There's no, like, UberEats or Deliveroo or whatever. So coming here to uni, it was a big, like, wow! I've actually got, like, stuff I can order. So yeah, I think I have definitely changed my habits.
Okay, fantastic! Have you ever considered ordering groceries through the delivery apps?
I have thought occasionally of it when I've, like, run out of, like, the normal stuff: like eggs, toast, pasta, that kind of stuff. But there is... I've got, like, a little Tesco Express that's only, like, five minutes down the road. And when I've, like, looked through ordering it to see how much it'd be, I think, well, I could just walk down to the shop. I don't need to be that lazy. But it has... I have thought about it. Yeah!
Nice. Okay. And what was the... what was the cost that made you, or put you off?
Yeah, I can't remember how much exactly it was, but I knew it'd be a lot more than just walking down to the shop. And it was... yeah, the cost pretty much put me off.
Was that because of the cost of each of the products, or was that the cost of the service charge? Like, what was the...?
I don't think the products themselves are too bad, but, like, on top of it, you've always got, like, the delivery charge and then the service charge. Then that sort of adds up to, like, five or six quid by themselves, and then you're looking at spending 15, 16 quid on something you didn't really need to buy.
Interesting... And do you not get free delivery with Uber One?
I... on certain shops, you do. I don't know. You have to spend, like, a minimum amount in order to get the free delivery. So when I signed up to, I thought it was initially great, and it is good, but you have to spend, like, a decent amount in order to get money off... if that makes sense.
Yeah... and do you not normally hit those thresholds?
I do, normally... when or in takeaways? Yeah.
Okay... but not with groceries.
No, not with groceries... Great!
What do you think would make the perfect grocery delivery app for students?
I think it's a good question. I think the main, like... I don't know, benefit of it would probably be money-saving, like... or sort of like a Travago. I think in, like, sort of comparing the prices from the shops you could order from and recommending you the best deals in order to save money.
Nice... okay, I suppose on that. Would promotions and discounts make you consider grocery delivery?
I think so... yeah, definitely!
What... what promotion or offer would you consider? Like, what's the perfect promotion look like for you that would entice you?
I think probably just a bit like... probably like 50, 40, 50, 60% off. Probably make me think twice about going to the shop or something when I know I could just order that quickly and get a good load of groceries for not too bad of a price.
Okay, I'm interested. You've only mentioned Uber so far, so I'm interested to know if delivery and Just Eat ever come into play.
I have used them, and I have got them on my phone. I've got the delivery student, like Silver Plus... I can't remember what it's called. Yeah, and I have used just D. But where I normally order from McDonald's a lot, and I know they're like Uber's primary person—primary, like, delivery service—so I normally tend to go towards them. But I have used Deliveroo and Just Eat in the past.
Okay, great! Have you seen anything from delivery apps that stood out to you recently?
Not really, no. I can't say I see them much... I see the Just Eat adverts on TV, but on social media, I can't say I really see that much from them. Okay?
So, what would you say tends to matter most to you in the moment when you order? Are there five things to list to you? So, is it price, is it offers and promotions, is it speed, is it habit, or finally, subscription benefits?
I'd probably say it's a mix of price—pardon me—and habit. Because I usually tend to order from the same sort of places, and I know what I'm getting, and I know the prices sort of... stay the same. So, I'd probably say those are the most important things for me when I'm ordering.
Okay, I want you to rate now each of these: the delivery services, or at least delivery, and Uber Eats, out of one to five. So, one is extremely terrible... one, or five, is extremely good. How would you rate delivery for price out of five?
I'd say... probably about a three.
And... what about Uber?
Four.
What about offers and promotions?
For Uber for delivery? Delivery? I'd probably go a 2, as I just tend... don't tend to see that many. Whether it's not because I... whether it's because I don't use it as much, I just don't tend to see that many promotions on there. And Uber... Uber, probably because I use it more, I see quite a decent amount of promotions on there, whether that be on the actual, like, Uber rides itself or UberEats.
Nice... Okay, and what about speed for delivery?
Probably a four...
Okay... and Uber?
Yeah... about the same. I'd probably say four.
And what about... habit?
Probably 4.
I'd say... for Uber or delivery?
For Uber and delivery... probably two or three.
And what about subscription benefits...?
Or, I say for Uber, probably a four, and then for delivery, a two. Because before, I've, like, done the same order on the two different apps, and whether or not because my student subscription knocked a bit more off, it was cheaper to do it on Uber than it was to do it on delivery.
Interesting... Okay, how often do you do the, the price comparison, and how often, then, does Uber win?
I do it very occasionally. I did it a couple of times when I was sort of getting to grips with the stuff I was ordering... But now I sort of just know that I prefer Uber, so I just sort of use that one. Okay?
So, in this case, when you're doing the price comparison, if a restaurant is on both apps—let's use McDonald's as the example—and it's a penny cheaper on delivery, what do you do?
I'd probably go with delivery at that point. I'm trying to be as economical as possible at the minute. So, yeah, I think I'd probably go with delivery.
Right, so the... the pound extra off is... is worth the... the effort of doing the price comparison?
Yes... yeah, I'd say so.
Great, thank you. So, you said you were subscribed to Uber and delivery. What made you subscribe to both?
Well, I... I was. The Uber One came to me just through a notification. When I was on my lock screen, it popped up and said that you've qualified for a, like, free 4-month or 5-month Uber One subscription. So I thought I'd take that and just see what it was about. And the delivery one came advertised to me through my... I can't remember what... Student Beans app. It was, like, advertised in the food section. It's like a free student account for that.
Nice. And how did you find Uber and delivery services compared to...?
I'd say, across the board, they're sort of the same. I think Uber is a slight bit quicker, but I'd probably say, across the board, they're about the same.
Okay, interesting... What does having a subscription change for you, if anything?
Apart from knocking money off... and with Uber One, when like... So, I sometimes use Uber itself to get to and from places around Southampton. I get cash back from that, like, put into credit in my account. So, it's more of a... like, I'm more inclined to use that than I would if it was delivery.
Okay, interesting... So, because you're using it as a taxi service, it's...
Yeah, yeah.
Okay... increasing that. Okay. Do you think you have saved money from having the subscriptions?
I don't know. That's interesting... I think I probably have. I mean, because sometimes I've like gone to order stuff, and then it says... And it comes to like, I don't know, 17, 18 quid. And then if you like apply one of the discounts or do one of the special promotions, it will take money off while still getting you the same product. So I'd say I probably have, but it'd be quite interesting to look at the real... Absolutely.
Okay... how often do you feel like you need to order to make the subscription worth it?
I probably say "probably" two to three times a week.
And... do you think that the benefits of the subscriptions are clear?
I think on Uber, they're definitely just... sort of the way the app itself is laid out. It's a bit more clear what the rewards are without... whereas on Deliveroo, you know, you've got the thing, but it doesn't... it's not as prevalent to you what you're actually getting off?
Absolutely. What I want to do now... I want to... I want to read out some benefits of the food delivery app subscriptions. So, I have six items to read out. I want you to pick your top three. So, the first one is free delivery. The second one is always on offer. So, for example, like 10% always off of McDonald's.
Yeah.
Third is grocery offers. Fourth is retail partners, like Boots. Fifth is other partnerships, like transport and lifestyle discounts. And then the final one is cashback.
I'd say free delivery is definitely number one. I always look to see which has got the best, like, delivery... because some places do charge you, whereas other places don't. Cashback is definitely ranked number two. And then third... Sorry, could you run me through?
Yeah... so, always on offers: grocery offers, retail partners like Boots, and other partnerships like transport and lifestyle discounts.
I'd probably say the transport discounts as my last one because I use... I use the trains a lot, so I'd probably say that one.
Great. So, you've got free delivery, cash back... then other partnerships.
Transport. Yeah.
Okay. Which out of those was the... the one that felt most irrelevant to you?
Probably the retail partnerships one...
Why is that?
Just sort of like... it's good, but it's sort of just like an added extra, I think, rather than like free delivery and money off. You know, you're getting it off in that order. Whereas in the retail partnerships one, it probably takes you to another link, to another app, and then you have to sort of path around with that.
Absolutely... Okay. Your top 'almost free' delivery—why was it the top choice?
Mostly just money saving, to be honest. Because I know on UberEats—I can't remember what it's like on Delivery—but they always say how much the delivery charge is, like sort of at the top of your order. So you're always pretty conscious of which places do and don't have a bigger charge for delivery.
Nice... Are there any benefits that I didn't list, that you would love to have?
I do think about sort of where delivery apps would have, like, a sort of... I use Tesco Clubcard as an example, where you sort of build up points in order to, like, get money off or that sort of thing. I sort of think that'd be interesting. But I know each app does its own different way of doing things.
Absolutely... okay. What would you say would make food delivery subscriptions feel like they're actually designed for students?
I think just catering towards students, really, and providing, like, specific offers towards students where you have to have, like, a student ID or a student email so that you feel like it's more targeted at you than just an offer that everybody could get.
Absolutely. What is it about that targeting that makes it feel personal, or makes it feel like something that would be... that would encourage you?
Sort of where... like when... when it's advertised on student offer apps like UNiDAYS and Tuna Beans. When you see, like, a... I don't know, a big delivery company or a restaurant or something that's advertising a deal for students only, you think that you're getting a good deal and not many other people, apart from students, would be able to get that deal. So, you think you're saving a decent amount of money?
Absolutely. Okay... in the future, then, how do you expect your eating habits to change after uni?
I think, probably depending on where I live after... whether I go back home or move somewhere else, move to another city... I think it will really change based on where I go and what sort of... what I'm doing with my life, whether I've got a job or whether I'm still studying. I think that would be really important to how much I order because I'll always be conscious of how much money I'm spending.
Absolutely! And do you think you'll use delivery apps more or less?
I think probably more, to be honest, if I'm... if I've got a decent job or I'm making more money, I'll probably be more inclined to think that, oh, it's just another takeaway, it's just another delivery, I can afford it, it's all right. Whereas now, when I'm living as a student, I think that I've got to save more money and, you know, I can't really afford to be spending that much money on a takeaway when I need to spend it on other stuff.
Absolutely. With that being said, then, would you expect to be paying for a food delivery subscription then after... you know?
Probably... I reckon so, yeah.
And how would you decide which subscription to go with once all the student benefits are gone?
I think, which one's really going to give me the best deal? Where? Like you said, where I'm not a student anymore, so I really have to think about which one's actually giving me the best... and which ones I'm going to use the most.
Absolutely. Great! Thank you. I have some questions to ask you around delivery drivers and riders. So, I'm interested in what your general perceptions are on delivery app riders.
Bit of... It's a bit of a mix, to be honest. Sometimes they're driving, sometimes they're on bikes, sometimes they're on scooters. Occasionally, they're like a tiny bit slow... like on Uber or delivery. It sometimes asks you to find a different driver, but most of the time, they're pretty quick and pretty on time!
Okay, great. And do you know anybody that is or has been a... a delivery or UberEats rider?
I don't know anybody that was, but one of my mates back home, who's also at uni, when we used to go back home for the summer and Christmas... he would be a Domino's, like, delivery driver. And he said he really enjoyed it.
That's fantastic! Okay... would you ever consider doing a writer's work job or role?
He has. He did make me think about it, to be fair. He said, like, when I used to talk to him about it, he said it was really, like, pretty chilled out, you know. He just sticks some music on, drives around, drops off some pizzas, and that it was quite an enjoyable job, to be honest.
Would you have any concerns about becoming a writer?
I think, like, where it's not consistent... like in... like it's not like working in a shop or something. So, I don't know where the money would always come from, if that makes sense. Like, it's not like a salary, or you get paid per hour; it's by the job. So, I think that would definitely... that would make me think.
So, in your opinion, then... how would you compare it to the other part-time jobs and side hustles?
I mostly worked in retail, so I think it'd be a lot different from that because you're always... you're probably working unsociable hours, like probably through the night, and it's not like consistent employment. Although it probably would be quite relaxing to not have somebody always looking over your shoulder, and you're sort of your own boss at that time.
Absolutely... Okay. Is there anything that you think would make it more appealing as a job?
I don't think so... no. Okay.
I have a... I have a, like, a poster or flyer to... to show you. I just want to get your opinion on it. Let me just share my screen. Essentially, I want to understand if you find this appealing as a student.
Yeah, yeah... it is quite like the study, right? The repeated slogan at the top is quite... it does make you think because I could essentially be doing... like, I could just study in the day, and then at night, I could be doing one of my delivery orders. And that gave me quite a bit of money. So it does, yeah, make me think about it, definitely!
So, this is one...
Yeah, no, it does. It's with... especially with the massive QR code. It does make you think about it because, you know, you could just scan your phone and, like, apply to be a rider and probably get accepted, and you could be making quite decent money within, like, the next couple of months. So it does definitely make me think. Great!
Were there any concerns about anything on the flyer?
No. No.
Okay, so... final few questions, then. Which is the... the best delivery app, would you say, for... for students?
I'd say, personally, Uber. I just like the way the app looks. I like the deals that it gives, whether or not I use it more... but I feel like it gives me more deals based on how much I use it, and just the sort of rewards it gives you.
Fantastic! Okay, and if delivery apps understood students, what do you think they would do differently?
I think they prioritize sort of the... the best deal for students, or like the... the cheapest options, like rank... like sorting the delivery places on which are the best to get from and which give the most deals, and so that... like a whole student section, I think.
Nice... Okay. And is there anything you think that the delivery apps could do to feel more relevant to you in your life?
I think I don't really see much of them on social media. I don't really see what their, sort of like, promotions are. I only really see them when I open the app itself. I don't really... like, I occasionally get notifications saying that they've got, like, an offer on or something, but it won't really tempt me unless I'm in the app and sort of thinking about ordering one already. Yeah.
Okay, last question then. Is there any... is there anything about food or delivery that we haven't talked about, that you feel is relevant and you want to... you want to raise?
No, I don't think so... No... Great!
Okay, Evan, that's all the questions I have for you. I just want to thank you for your time, and thank you... One thing that I want to pass by you: we do have another study that's starting tomorrow. It's a diary study. So, what that looks like... it's a seven-day study where essentially you just keep track of what you're eating, and in the evening, you do like a... like a video log, like a blog, sort of quick video to tell us what you've... what you've been eating that day. Would that be something that would be interesting to you? It's a hundred pounds for the seven days!
Possibly... Yeah. Yeah, definitely. Great!
Well, what I'll do... I'll put your... I'll put your name down to the delivery team. They will reach out via this chat and essentially send you a link to apply for another study with Askable. And then once you apply in there, they'll send you all the instructions of what needs to be done and how it's... how it's done.
Cool.
Great.
Sweet! Thank you.
Brilliant. Evan, take care... and you have a good... cheers!
You too.
Cheers, boy.
All right.
